basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Infiṭār — “The Cleaving” — unfolds four images of apocalypse in four verses: the heaven cleaves, the stars scatter, the seas overflow, the graves are overturned. And then, in a flash, the sentence: every soul shall know what it has done and what it has left undone. Then comes the most singular apostrophe in the Quran to the sinner: O man, what has deceived you concerning your most generous Lord? And Qushayrī plunges into the extreme delicacy of this question. He imagines that the question itself contains its answer, like a secret breathed by divine generosity: « It is Your generosity itself that deceived me; without Your generosity, I would not have committed the fault, for You see and You cover, You measure and You grant the delay. » And he then cites one of his small poems — one of the rare passages in which he speaks in his own name: « My Master says: Are you not ashamed of the ugliness of your acts? — I say: O my Master, be gentle, for the abundance of Your favours has emboldened me. » The sūra closes on sovereignty: the Affair, that Day, belongs to God. Always His, certainly; but on that Day, no human pretension will any longer mask it.

versets.commentaire: infaṭarat (انْفَطَرَتْ) — Qushayrī, in one word: “it has split.” Same root as fiṭra (original nature): the heaven returns, in cleaving, to its primordial state — that of before cosmic ordering.

mots.def: infaṭara: to split, to cleave. Same root as fiṭra.

versets.commentaire: intatharat: they have scattered, like the beads of a broken necklace. The very structure of the heaven — its ornament (cf. sūra 85) — comes undone.

mots.def: kawākib: plural of kawkab; stars, planets.

versets.commentaire: fujjirat: have been burst open, one into another. Qushayrī: “opened one into the other.” The seas, hitherto separated by barriers, merge.

mots.def: fajjara in the passive; to make gush, to open.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the graves shall be overturned,
versets.commentaire: buʿthirat — a verb already met in sūra 100. Qushayrī: “its earth is overturned, and the dead in it are raised; what it contained of treasures and dead is brought out.”

mots.def: buʿthira: to be overturned, to be tipped out.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: every soul shall know what it has accomplished and what it has deferred.
versets.commentaire: Response to the four preceding conditions. Qushayrī: “when these things come about, every soul knows what it has sent forward of good and evil.” Qaddamat: what it has sent ahead — its accomplished works. Akhkharat: what it has left behind — its delayed works, its postponements, the seeds it never planted.

versets.ar: يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنسَانُ مَا غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ الْكَرِيمِ
versets.tr: yā ayyuhā l-insānu mā gharraka bi-rabbika l-karīm
versets.trad: O you, man! What then has deceived you concerning your most generous Lord,
versets.commentaire: A prodigious verse. mā gharraka: what has deceived you? Qushayrī: “what has illusioned you, what has seduced you, to the point that you have acted in His disobediences?” And he offers the most delicate reading in the Quran: « He has questioned him as though the question itself contained the answer. He said: Your generosity deceived me; without Your generosity, I would not have done it, because You see and You cover, You measure and You grant the delay. » The sinner acknowledges: it is because You are generous that I have been emboldened to sin. And Qushayrī then cites one of his small poems — one of the rare passages where he speaks in his own name: « My Master says: Are you not ashamed of what I see of the ugliness of your acts? / I say: O my Master, be gentle, for the abundance of Your favours has emboldened me. »

mots.def: gharra: to deceive, to lure; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: Who created you, then modelled and harmoniously fashioned you?
versets.commentaire: Three verbs for the creation of man: khalaqa (to create), sawwā (to harmonise), ʿadala (to balance). This last one says the equity of the composition: no part is in excess, none lacking. The human body is, in essence, just.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: sawwā: to harmonise; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ʿadala: to balance, to render just; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.ar: فِي أَيِّ صُورَةٍ مَّا شَاءَ رَكَّبَكَ
versets.tr: fī ayyi ṣūratin mā shāʾa rakkabak
versets.trad: He has composed you in whatever form He has willed.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “He has composed your members along the paths of wisdom, in whatever form He has willed — beauty or homeliness, length or shortness.” And he opens a deeper reading: ṣūra here may signify the inward form, the spiritual state. In whichever state He has willed to place you — happiness or wretchedness, faith or disobedience — He has set you there.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: On the contrary, you treat the Judgment as a lie
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “the Judgment, that is, the Resurrection.” After the reminder of favours, the point of denial: one has received all this grace — and one holds the Judgment as a lie.

versets.trad: while keepers are set over you
versets.commentaire: ḥāfiẓīn: keepers. Qushayrī: “these are the angels who write down the deeds. He frightened them by the sight of the angels and their writing of deeds — yet if they truly knew the matter, their guarding against transgressions, through the sight of the True and shame before His gaze, would be more complete than through the sight of the angels.” Subtle: modesty before God Himself ought to be stronger than modesty before the scribe-angels. But since we measure only what is visible, God speaks to us first of the visible.
kirām kātibīn: noble scribes. Quranic tradition of the two angels who inscribe good deeds on the right and bad deeds on the left.
A simple, and formidable, phrase. Their knowing has no delay. At the very moment the act is done, it is already known.

versets.trad: Truly, the just shall be in a place of delight
versets.commentaire: A verse anticipating the finale of sūra 83. But Qushayrī here gives the present reading: “The just are today in the breath of dhikr, in intimacy at their hours of seclusion.” Naʿīm does not begin tomorrow; it begins this very day in the heart that remembers.

versets.trad: and the libertines in a furnace
versets.commentaire: And Qushayrī gives the present reading, in mirror: “the libertines are in the narrowness of their hearts and in their displeasure with the decree, in the darkness of their planning (tadbīr, تَدْبِير) and the narrowness of their choosing (ikhtiyār, اخْتِيَار).” The jaḥīm of the libertine also begins down here: it is the inner narrowness of one who wants to control everything.

versets.trad: where they shall burn the Day of Judgment
versets.commentaire: The Fire of the Day is the visible manifestation of the Fire already lit in the heart.

mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

versets.trad: without being able to absent themselves from it.
versets.commentaire: ghāʾibīn: absent. They shall have no power to absent themselves from the Fire. The verse names the opposite of usual human evasion: here, one can no longer look away.

versets.trad: But what will let you know what the Day of Judgment is?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “a formula uttered with amplifying value (tahwīl).”

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.ar: ثُمَّ مَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا يَوْمُ الدِّينِ
versets.tr: thumma mā adrāka mā yawmu d-dīn
versets.trad: Once again: what will let you know what the Day of Judgment is?
versets.commentaire: The repetition is not redundancy: it underlines, as in verses 5-6 of sūra 94, that the matter stated deserves to be heard twice. The amplification repeats itself so that the ear may halt at it.

versets.ar: يَوْمَ لَا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِّنَفْسٍ شَيْئًا ۖ وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلَّهِ
versets.tr: yawma lā tamliku nafsun li-nafsin shayʾan wa-l-amru yawmaʾidhin li-Llāh
versets.trad: That Day, no soul shall have any power to help another. And the decision, that day, belongs to God!
versets.commentaire: An absolute closing verse. Qushayrī: “The Affair belongs to God on that Day, and belongs to Him before and after. But on that Day, the pretensions fall, for the matter becomes clear, and knowings become necessary” — that is, they impose themselves on all without being refusable any longer. Today one can deny; tomorrow one will know, and one will no longer be able to undo it. Knowing becomes a forced evidence. The sūra, opened upon the heaven that cleaves, closes on this evidence that does not cleave.

mots.def: malaka: to possess, to have power.
